talk_title: Clinical application of radiopharmaceutical theranostics in neuroendocrine tumors and prostate cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Jonathan McConathy
speaker.affiliation: University of Alabama, Department of Radiology

biomarkers.name: SSTR2 PET uptake (DOTATATE SUV)
biomarkers.finding: Higher tumor SUV correlated with improved progression‑free survival in NETTER‑1

biomarkers.name: PSMA PET SUVmean >10
biomarkers.finding: Patients with SUVmean >10 had higher PSA response rates in PSMA‑targeted trials

biomarkers.name: Serum PSA
biomarkers.finding: Median PSA decline >50% observed after three cycles of PSMA‑617 in responsive patients

models.name: NETTER‑1
models.genotype_or_key_features: Grade 1‑2 GEP‑NETs progressed on somatostatin analog

models.name: VISION
models.genotype_or_key_features: Metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer after ARPI and taxane

clinical_data.value: significant improvement vs high‑dose somatostatin analog

MOA_summary: Radiolabeled somatostatin analogs (DOTATATE) and PSMA‑targeted ligands (PSMA‑617) bind cell‑surface receptors on neuroendocrine tumors or prostate cancer cells, are internalized, and deliver beta‑particle radiation (Lu‑177) that induces DNA double‑strand breaks, leading to tumor cell death while allowing PET imaging of target expression for patient selection and dosimetry.
key_findings: DOTATATE therapy (NETTER‑1) provided a marked progression‑free survival benefit compared with high‑dose octreotide in Grade 1‑2 GEP‑NETs.
PSMA‑617 therapy (VISION) improved overall survival by 4 months versus standard of care in mCRPC after ARPI and taxane.
Higher baseline PSMA PET SUVmean (>10) predicted greater PSA response and longer PFS in PSMA‑targeted trials.
Patients with high circulating tumor DNA or PTEN loss had poorer outcomes on PSMA therapy, suggesting genomic biomarkers of resistance.
Combination of PSMA‑targeted radioligand therapy with PARP inhibitors or immune checkpoint blockade is feasible and shows early safety signals.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with low PSMA uptake or high FDG‑avid disease showed limited response to PSMA‑617.
PTEN alterations were associated with worse PFS on PSMA therapy, indicating a potential resistance mechanism.

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit DNA repair to potentiate radiation‑induced DNA damage
combination_strategies.phenotype: Early phase trials report acceptable safety and suggest enhanced tumor control

combination_strategies.rationale: Radiation may increase neoantigen release and immune activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Preliminary data show synergistic tumor regression in a subset of patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Increase dose to bulky or resistant lesions while systemic radioligand treats micrometastases
combination_strategies.phenotype: Palliative pain relief and improved local control reported in case series

open_questions: What is the optimal sequencing of beta‑ versus alpha‑emitting PSMA agents?
Can early on‑treatment imaging biomarkers (e.g., new lesions on interim PET) reliably predict long‑term survival?
How should dosing intervals be personalized based on hematologic recovery versus tumor dosimetry?
